Dopamine is often called the "feel-good" hormone because it's released in the brain when we experience pleasure or reward. lessons.title: Market Desires
lessons.description:
Slides:
https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Mv-wr2ePnnI_00myK11WHUXCj9bLZuKbohk9s83fwc0/edit?usp=sharing
Understanding Desires:Mass Desire = "The public spread of a private want"
When millions of people privately want the same thing, it becomes a mass desire that advertising can harness.
Key Rule: You cannot CREATE desires. You can only CHANNEL existing desires onto your product.
How Desires Are Born: The Two ForcesPermanent Forces (Always There)Mass Instincts: Universal human drives (health, attractiveness, security)Technological Problems: Ongoing issues (car repairs, slow internet)These create stable, recurring desires that never go awayForces of Change (Create New Desires)Style Trends: What becomes fashionable (swimming pools vs. bigger cars)Mass Education: What society learns (health awareness, new technologies)Cultural Shifts: Changes in values (women's liberation, environmental consciousness)These create emerging desires and shift existing onesThe Three Desire CategoriesOnce a desire exists (from the forces above), it falls into one of these types:
Physical Desires
Health, strength, attractiveness, freedom from pain, sensory satisfactionMaterial Desires
Money, possessions, status symbols, security, convenienceEmotional/Social Desires
Love, acceptance, recognition, success, power, adventureHow to Find & Evaluate DesiresStep 1: Spot the DesireWatch for complaint patternsStudy successful competitorsFollow cultural trendsLook where people spend moneyStep 2: Test Its Power (The 3 Dimensions)Urgency: How desperately do they want relief? Staying Power: Does this desire renew continuously? Scope: How many people share this desire?
Step 3: Choose the StrongestPick the desire with the highest combination of all three dimensions.
The Process in Action1. Forces Create Desire Example: Social media culture (Force of Change) → People want to look "Instagram-worthy" (New Mass Desire)
2. Categorize the Desire
"Instagram-worthy" = Physical desire (appearance) + Emotional desire (social recognition)
3. Channel onto Your Product Beauty brand: "Get that perfect selfie glow with our new foundation"
Schwartz's Key InsightsNever Fight Desires: The Edsel failed because it fought the mass desire for compact cars
Ride the Wave: Successful companies spot desires early and channel them onto their products
Timing is Everything: Same product can succeed or fail based on which desires are rising/falling
Specificity Wins: Don't just say "people want to be attractive" - find the specific way they want to be attractive right now
Your Job as a MarketerBe a Desire Detective: Constantly watch for emerging desires and shifting patternsPick Your Lane: Choose one powerful desire to focus onChannel, Don't Create: Direct existing desires onto your productStay Alert: Desires change - be ready to pivot when they doThe Bottom Line: Master this, and you can predict what people will want to buy before your competitors even know there's a market for it.

lessons.title: Rule Of 1 - 1 Desire, Avatar, Message
lessons.description:
This is one of the most important videos to watch so you fully grasp why you need to go deep into the Desires, Avatars, Angles, and make it congruent to your LP.

And also why your ad + LP should only appeal to 1 desire and 1 avatar to build more belief.

This is certified Chad scaling sauce.

Correction:
Angle = Emotional Trigger + [Daily Struggle OR Ideal Outcome]

Problem/Daily Struggle: Current pain + what makes it hell 
Aspirational/Ideal: Current emotion + dream state 
Your "meeting point" - where their feelings and your message connect

If you were not selling a problem solving product for example, like fashion. You would then show what your avatar aspires to be or look like.

The point is still the same. Per Schwartz's teachings, our goal is to be able to relate to our customers and enter their current emotional state through relating to their current state of emotions. And you can show that via a daily struggle or ideal outcome.

lessons.title: 🆕 Ad ONLY Instagram Research
lessons.description:
This is how to access the “ad side” of Instagram, so when you’re scrolling. you ONLY see ads
Step 1: Scroll the home page for a bit until you find a post that says “sponsored”
Step 2: Click on the creative
Step 3: Start scrolling
Step 4: Save any ads you like, or share them with yourself on IG, log into IG on your computer, copy the links and store them in a database, or download them on your computer and create a swipefile with a tool like: https://snapinsta.to/en2
Super easy and cracked way to do research.
I would encourage everyone to scroll ads for 10-15 mins per day, save ads you like/find interesting or share them in ⁠ad-inspo

PRO TIP: 
What you’ll also notice is how fast you scroll determines what type of ads you get, which is super interesting.
If I scroll faster for a day and just run through ads, the next day I’ll get shorter ads. If I take the time to watch through most of the ads, I’ll get longer ads and VSLs

s/o Karlo

lessons.title: Customer Phone Call Research
lessons.description:
Call a customer who has placed a higher than average purchase order and follow a script along the lines of this
Hey NAME, it's YOUR NAME calling from YOUR BRAND, is now a bad time to talk?
Yes =  Make sure you schedule a time to call back
No = Move forward
I saw you just placed an order with us, and I wanted to, 1. personally thank you for supporting our brand, and 2. Just ask if you could explain a little bit more about how you got to our brand.

Let them talk for as long as possible.
You're looking into better understand their journey what they were looking for, and some of the things that they were potentially going through that ultimately made them purchase your product.

lessons.title: 🆕 Understanding Your Product - Part 3
lessons.description:
Expedite the process with Claude.ai custom projects to fill out the product document for you.
Prompt:
Hello, I would like to create a custom project tailored to assist our marketing agency in creating more effective, data-driven ads for our client, an E commerce Brand. Brand name: BRAND Brand website: YOURWEBSITE The purpose of this custom project is to support the following key marketing functions for this brand: Customer Understanding & Market Trends: Analyze customer surveys, buyer personas, and behavioral data to uncover customer preferences, pain points, and purchasing motivations.Identify market trends and audience segments relevant to this brand’s products. Market Research & Competitive Analysis: Gather and analyze data on competitors and broader industry trends. Identify opportunities for differentiation in brand’s positioning and advertising strategies. Creative Ideation & Strategy: Generate new creative concepts and strategies for ad campaigns based on insights from market data, customer feedback, and competitive analysis. Provide feedback on existing ideas, suggesting improvements grounded in customer insights and market trends. Scriptwriting & Copy Development: Assist with crafting persuasive, targeted scripts for ads and other marketing materials. Review and critique copy-writing to ensure it resonates with the target audience, incorporating customer pain points and desires. Ad Review & Performance Feedback Loop: Assist in analyzing post-launch ad performance by reviewing metrics such as ad spend, ROAS, hook rate, hold rate, CTR, etc. Provide insights on why certain ads performed well or underperformed, identifying key learnings from each campaign. Close the feedback loop by suggesting actionable takeaways from winning and losing ads, incorporating these learnings into future creative strategies. Knowledge Base: This project should use the attached files as its primary knowledge base and refer to the information within when providing insights or recommendations. Whenever this project provides an answer, it should be informed by the content within the knowledge base. It should autonomously apply the insights from the attached files to ensure all responses are grounded in data and relevant context. Key Responsibilities: Interpret customer feedback and survey data to identify trends and actionable insights. Suggest ad strategies, creative angles, and copy that align with market research and customer insights. Ensure all insights, scripts, and suggestions are data-driven and relevant to brand’s target audience, drawing on the knowledge base provided. Analyze ad performance post-launch, using key metrics to provide insights into ad success or failure. Instructions: Always ground your responses in the content within the knowledge base. Highlight any recommendations based on specific insights or principles from the provided data or resources, especially when drawing on "Breakthrough Advertising." Prioritize relevance to brand’s audience, and use data to back up your suggestions."

lessons.title: 🆕 I'm Overwhelmed /Stuck - Wat Do?
lessons.description:
First, what is the definition?
Definition: 
Bury or drown beneath a huge mass.
Our Definition:
Give too much of a thing to (someone)...
In other words..


What You Have To Do:
Study → Research → Create → Test → Feedback LoopStudy: Learning new information to improve understanding & identify the unknown
Research: Figure out what people want and why they want it.
Create: Communicate via ads, pages, offers how you can help those people get what they want
Test: Launch tests in 1 campaign. If it gets spend = good. If it does not get spend = not good.
Feedback Loop: Make educated guesses on why something is working or not working and keep track of those learnings.

Here Is The Important Part:
Not Finding Winners? --> Spend more time on feedback loops to figure out execution flaws
Still Not Finding Winners? --> Go back to researching again to ensure your understanding is clear
Still Not Finding Winners No Matter What You Do? --> Go back and study information again to improve your understanding & identify what you don't know and/or what you are missing  (Reminded more than taught)
That's It.

lessons.title: 🆕 Understanding Ad Definitions
lessons.description:
Ad Definitions:
Please note that the definitions that follow are the ones we have chosen to use. 

At the end of the day, I want you to use the definitions that make the most amount of sense to you.
But hopefully these help guide you so we can think less about terms and philosophies and just focus on pure execution.

💻3:2:2 ⇒ 3 creatives, 2 body copies, and 2 headlines
12-Year-Old Definition: The testing structure we use inside of Meta. 
Example:
This ENTIRE thing is a Concept. “3” = Variations of that 1 Concept. 
“2:2” = 2 Headline + 2 Body copy for that Concept that you put inside of Meta.


📂Concept/Batch/Test #:⇒ The tracking number for this test
12-Year-Old Definition: The number you use to keep track of which test you're on
Examples: 
Batch #1, Batch #29, Batch #133
💡Concept/Swing ⇒ The test strategy - what you're testing in this batch
12-Year-Old Concept Definition: The big idea behind the ads you want to test
Examples: 
"Test an iteration on a winning video."
"Test superlative angles."
"Test UGC format"
"Test different avatars."
🔗Angle⇒The selling strategy - how you're choosing to sell the product
12-Year-Old Angle Definition: The main reason you're giving someone to buy 

Examples:
"Your child will enjoy taking their greens" - Grüns
"It will help your digestive system" - Grüns
"You won't be drinking microplastics" - Liquid Death
"You'll look cool while you drink us" - Liquid Death
Key insight: Every ad needs an angle. Even if your concept is "testing formats," you still need to define what you're selling. If your concept is “Testing X Angle” the concept becomes the angle. But for tracking purposes, I prefer to actually JOT DOWN the angle twice so we can be sure.
🔀Variations ⇒ Individual executions of the angle(s)
12-Year-Old Variations Definition: Different ways to communicate your selling reason(s)
Example: 
If your angle is "Buy It For Life," variations might be: "One harness, lifetime of adventures," "Buy it once, adventure for life," or "End the replacement cycle"
If testing multiple angles, variations might be different angles like "Stainless steel," "Doctor-designed," "Easiest to clean."


🖼️Format ⇒ How you deliver the ad
12-Year-Old Format Definition: The type of ad you're making
Example: You could show the same message as a video of a real person talking, a slideshow of pictures, or just a product image.

lessons.title: How To Set Up a 3:2:2 Campaign
lessons.description:
Easy A UTM code:
utm_source=facebook&utm_medium=paid&utm_campaign={{campaign.name}}&utm_term={{adset.name}}&utm_content={{ad.name}}&fbadid={{ad.id}}&tw_source={{site_source_name}}&tw_adid={{ad.id}}

To Sum Up:
1 Campaign
1 Champions Ad set, Multiple Creative Test Ad Sets
3 creatives in each ad set (same concept), each with 2 headlines and 2 copies

⚠ WARNING DO NOT TURN OFF YOUR EXISTING ADS IF THEY ARE WORKING. THE DUMBEST THING YOU CAN DO IS TURN OFF ADS THAT ARE WORKING. IF YOUR OTHER CAMPAIGNS ARE WORKING - LEAVE THEM ON - IF YOU TURN THEM OFF AND YOU LOSE MONEY... THAT IS ON YOU.

FAQ:
How many creative ad sets can you launch at a time?
If you're only spending few hundred a day, I recommend keeping it to 5 ad sets (including champions ad set, if it has ads inside) at a time so budget can be distributed efficiently. Focus more on intent of the ads instead of throwing things at the wall.You can launch all 5 ad sets in the same day if you wishIf you want the exact calculation on how to figure out the max number of creatives to test per week:
Example #1 - Budget $100/day
Total Budget (example $100/day)
Target CPA (Example $20)
Total Ads = Budget/TCPA
$100/$20 = 5
So the maximum amount of concepts is 5, considering 100% of your budget is testing.If you're spending a few thousand a day you can definitely have more ad sets due to budget and CPADo we set min/max spend limits for this campaign method?
No, we tested it and it didn't help.How long do you test creatives for?
We recommend testing creatives for a minimum of 3 days. However, we now leave them testing 7 days. If they don't do well after 7 days (not much spending or missing KPI), we will then turn them off. If you want to test ads longer, you can. But we usually don't recommend leaving ads on for longer than 14 days.How do you know if a creative ad set is a winner (Covered in "When To Leave a 3:2:2 Running" video below)?
If it gets majority of spend at KPI like shown in the video. Besides that if you're still not sure, the question you should ask is "Did my overall campaign performance improve?". If your overall campaign ROAS didn't improve, it's not a winner.When do you turn off creatives/ad sets (Covered in "When To Turn Off A 3:2:2" video below)?
With this method we are only turning off at the ad set level not ad level.If the ad set is still not getting much spend after 7 days, and ROAS is not good we will turn off. If ROAS is good you may leave it running if you have no other creatives in queue.If the ad set took majority of spend within the first 3 days, performance is not good and overall campaign performance went down, we will turn off. But we will do learnings on the ad because something about it got a lot of engagement, so make it more conversion driven. Try to give it 2-3 days to test, it may not convert that well 1st day but 2nd or 3rd day pick up.No we don't turn off ads that were doing well but suddenly tanked, zoom out look at averages insteadWhat's the minimum budget to launch this?
Minimum I would start this campaign at is $100/dayWhat if I don't have any past winners to put in the champions ad set or it's a new brand
You can set up the champions ad set still just don't launch any ads inside there until you have a proven winner you can drop inside.Can you mix images and videos in this same campaign?
Yes, that is fine but keep them separate ad sets. 1 concept should be 1 ad set, so 1 image concept with 3 variations.

This campaign is a CBO Cost Cap Campaign for ads that have “failed.”
Our goal with this campaign is a low-maintenance way to try to get "more" out of each creative test without burning budget.
This campaign will prioritize "failed ads" that can achieve the best CPAs and attempt to generate an ROI from each creative test.
We have also found (in the seasonal ads section) that, based on the market and things that happen in the real world, desires will shift, and Meta may favor ads that "failed" during specific testing periods, but now have an opportunity to drive some sales.

lessons.title: 🆕 How To Set Up ABO Scaling Campaign
lessons.description:
Time Stamps:
0:20 - Important Rule Before Doing This
1:15 - Live Ad Account Data/Proof
2:30 - ABO Scaling Campaign Setup
6:39 - What Budget?
7:45 - Still Use The Champions Adset?
9:00 - ABO Testing Campaign (Not Creatives)
11:20 - Performance From Attribution/Exclusion Tests
13:20 - New Recent $25k/day Breakthrough Ad
14:37 - What Meta Will Do With Overlap
15:35 - Live Walkthrough Of "How To Setup"

THIS WILL NOT FIX YOUR BAD ADS.IF YOU ARE LESS THAN $1K/DAY IN SPEND, FOCUS MORE ON WINNING ADS.ALSO, IF YOU DO NOT HAVE A MEDIA BUYER, WE RECOMMEND GETTING ONE BEFORE DOING THIS.MANAGING THIS MANY AD SETS REQUIRES MORE TIME & EFFORT.
ABO Testing/Scaling Campaign (Recommended)#1 - Breakthroughs get their own ad set to scale further
#2 - Spend Winners get their own ad set to throttle their spend
#3 - KPI winners get their own ad set to see if they can take on more spend
^ NEVER TURN OFF ANY OF THESE ADS IN THE MAIN CBO AFTER YOU DUPLICATE TO ABO (unless obviously performance is terrible, which is another video)
#4 - Losers will move to zombie campaign
These ad sets will have the same setup as the CBO ad sets

#2 - What budget do you start these at?5-20% of total daily budget → remember you can always scale up (lower on initial spend on kpi winners, higher initial spend on breakthroughs)

#3 - Are we still using a champions ad set?No.

ABO Testing Only Campaign (Optional)Again, a separate campaign is OPTIONAL for this. We are currently just adding these as individual adsets in our main testing/scaling ABO campaign.

Assuming the main CBO campaign is running on 7-day click attribution:
Ad Set 1: Hard Exclusions
Test the top ads again under 7-day click, but go very aggressive on exclusions. Exclude website visitors, add-to-carts, page engagers (365 days), past customers — basically every possible interaction a user might have with the brand
The goal here is to force Facebook to go after net new people instead of spending on existing audience segments, since the main campaign is already doing that.
Ad Set 2: 1 Day Click Attribution (Testing OR Scaling)
Test the same top ads under 1-day click attribution, but with more relaxed exclusions (only exclude purchasers).
1DC is naturally more conservative and tends to limit over-attribution, so we don’t need to be as aggressive with exclusions here.

Ad Set 3: Incremental Attribution (Testing OR Scaling)
Test the same top ads using incremental attribution settings, again with very strong exclusions to push Facebook toward net new acquisition.

IN FINAL
This isn’t our core campaign.
The main CBO is still cruising and protecting baseline revenue. So even if one of these aggressive tests breaks, the overall business performance won’t break with it.
This structure lets us push growth without destabilizing the foundation.

lessons.title: How To Set Targets & KPIs
lessons.description:
There should only be 3 KPI's that you look at in your ad account. The video above will show you how to calculate based on ROAS:
https://easyamedia.com/profit-calculator/
(Beginner) KPI #1: BROAS = Blended Return On Ad Spend
Breakeven BROAS = The minimum amount in revenue your store needs to make  to be profitable.
Scaling BROAS = The BROAS you are comfortable increasing budgets at.

(Intermediate) KPI #2: ROAS = Return On Ad Spend
Breakeven ROAS = The Minimum amount you need back to be profitable.
Scaling ROAS = The ROAS you are comfortable increasing budgets at.

(Advanced) KPI #3: CPA or NCPA and LTV = Cost Per Acquisition or New Customer Cost Per Acquisition and Life Time Value (TripleWhale needed)
Breakeven CPA = The maximum amount you can spend to acquire a customer
Scaling CPA = The ideal amount you are spending to acquire a customer
LTV = Lifetime value of the customer based on specific time periods.
https://shorturl.at/5Qori

🆕 For an advanced training on how to set KPIs based on LTV, please watch Grayson's video here:https://www.skool.com/evolve-8484/classroom/67ce5630?md=c3968f95f6f04e149a1544673ab39c87

lessons.title: How To See If You Should Turn Off High-Spend Ads
lessons.description:
Time Stamp: 3:49 - JUST AS A NOTE - WE SHOULD KILL BATCH #2 - the reason we DID NOT KILL THIS is because I am forcing my media buyer to test everything for 7-days (you guys don't need to do this - it's just an experiment). But this would be a great example of: "Champions is getting 4.6x ROAS with $2.5k, Batch #2 is getting 1.74x with 3.9k ... So, we should KILL Batch #2 and let that spend go to the champions + other ads because the champions ad with $1.4k more spend (or a 60% increase) will likely still be better than a 1.74x
If you still need support on this, I also have a YouTube video that goes pretty in-depth linked here inside of the Origins Program:
https://www.skool.com/evolve-8484/classroom/e9123143?md=46e2f8359af44dd8bf18a81162d74764

Decision-Making Framework For Turning Off High Spending Ads:

1. What is the frequency? Low frequency  (1.5 or below) = prospecting2. How much is the ad spending & what is the ROAS / CPA? --> From there, if I was to turn off this ad and therefore force that spend to my other top 2-3 ads, would their performance be better than the top spender? 

^ Most of the time the answer to that is NO. If you believe it is a "yes" then turn it off

3. If a NEW ad starts sucking up spend and gets shit KPI, you can turn it off in the first 24-48 hours. If you are spending $20k/day you should have rules in place to avoid getting burned.

Context:

We turn off ads that are not getting spend AND NOT hitting KPI after 7 days+ 

The reason being, we want each ad set ideally to be able to get 3x CPA worth of spend 

For $30 target CPA each ad set should get $90 spend per day 

So if you had $900/d you should have 10 ad sets max running 

But you want to keep testing things 

So the ones that are performing the worst you turn off to make space for new creative tests
 
Which would be first, ads that are not taking spend AND not at KPI.

lessons.title: Why the fact it’s hard is actually good
lessons.description:
If something were easy, everyone would have it. Difficulty is a filter that separates those who want it from those who deserve it.The “hardness” of something is what creates scarcity. Scarcity = value.“The fact that most people quit is the reason you shouldn’t.”
- Someone smarter than meEmotional FramingEvery frustration (No winning ads, low ROAS, losing money) is proof that you’re in the arena. Most aren’t.Instead of saying “this is hard,” say: “This is the part that makes it worth it.”
That mindset flips pain into proof of progress.The very reason most people don’t have it, is the same reason it’s so rewarding when you finally do.The fact that it’s hard is what makes it worth it.
- Mozi
...Also, it’s not really that hard lol...

🕰️ Then vs. Now — “Starting a Business in the 1990s vs. 2020s”THEN (1990s)
Had to rent office space — even if you were solo.Needed landlines, fax machines, and physical business cards.Building a website? Cost $10K–$50K and took months.Marketing? Yellow Pages, newspaper ads, or local radio.To reach customers outside your city, you needed a warehouse, shipping partners, and trust built through mail-order catalogs.To hire? You posted in local classifieds. Couldn’t tap into global talent.Raising money meant begging banks or investors for a loan and wearing a suit.Scaling beyond your town required logistics, not just clicks.NOW (2020s+)
You can start a brand from your couch with $1,000 and a laptop.No-code tools (Shopify, Webflow, Zapier) let you launch a full business in a day.You can test ideas with AI, market research, ad copy, product names, instantly.You can hire people from the Philippines, Pakistan, or Eastern Europe for a fraction of local cost.You can reach 10,000,000 people overnight with one viral video or ad for FREE.You can receive instant feedback from comments, analytics, or surveys.You can sell globally, automatic translations, global payment processors, fulfillment handled by Amazon or 3PLs.You can learn everything for free on YouTube, X, or inside communities like Origins for less than $100.You can literally talk to an AI team 24/7 to help you brainstorm, design, and execute.In 1995, if you wanted to start a supplement brand, you’d need to convince a manufacturer to take your call, spend $30K on product runs, $10K on design, and pray you could get your product into local stores.
In 2025, you can get a Shopify template, prompt Claude for your product description, hire a designer on Fiverr, and run your first ad on Meta all before lunch.💡 Data & Historical ExamplesAmazon took three years to hit $1M/month in revenue.
→ Many Shopify stores hit that in less than 6 months today.Nike’s first ad in the ‘70s cost $10,000 and reached thousands.
→ A single UGC video can reach millions organically.Hiring developers in 1995: $80K+ salary, on-site.
→ Hiring developers in 2025: $2K/month, remote from anywhere.

lessons.title: Live Product Research w/ Kalodata - Pt 2
lessons.description:
Note:
To clarify, this does not mean you should rip ads and hope for the best. You have to make your own ads together based on the following modules. If you don't figure out how to stand out beforehand and create your own ads to do so, you will not build any skills.

Checklist from the video:
1) For this method we're using a paid tool Kalodata. Kalodata is a tool that pulls the best performing TikTok shop products. You can get access to it for cheaper via Toolsuite
2) Filter at the product level. Last 7 days, Avg unit price >$15, sales growing rapidly.
3) Any of the products here not attached to a big brand (E.g. Goli, Shark, Comfrt) has the potential to be sold.
4) Go through the listings and look into the brand to verify if it is a big brand. Do a Google search, check their social media following. If they're a established brand they can easily afford litigation to take you down. Not worth it running bootleg versions.

5) Check the trademark websites on wherever you plan to sell to verify if the brand/product name has a trademark. For example in the US you may use https://tmsearch.uspto.gov/search/search-information (Free) or GPT/Claude to find out. If the product is called "Blood Elixir" and all the TT videos mention it, if you reuse those clips with product name mention there's a higher risk of getting taken down. In that case obviously use a different brand name and also different product name. Trademarked doesn't mean you can't sell the product, just means you can't use certain phrases on the site and ads.
6) Use Similarweb (via Toolsuite) to estimate how much the store is doing. Always be more conservative. Let's say the average is 1% conversion rate (CVR). Estimate the AOV based on product selling price. Website visitors x CVR x AOV = Estimated Revenue. For this prod research method, the estimated revenue on the site is less important as some stores do a lot with TTS but not on their own website. Product can still sell via FB ads.
7) Do some basic research on the product. Go through the top performing videos from Kalodata TikTok shop videos and the comments. See what's the current angle they're targeting, who they're targeting, what the comments are saying.

8) Also go through https://www.facebook.com/ads/library and Atria (via Toolsuite) to see what the competition is running.

9) Do more research based on the subsequent modules on how to find your avatar. Wherever possible - Google search, YT videos, IG reels, Tiktok videos. We know there's only 2 ways to win if you're not first to market. A new mechanism or an underserved avatar. The subsequent module will cover the avatar side. Our goal with this research is to find if we can find new angles, mechanisms, or avatars we can target besides what the competition is doing so you can stand out. So we need to know what types of angles your current competition is running and the current market sophistication level.

10) Check with the supplier (supplier here) if the pricing they can get makes sense. You want to use tools like Claude, GPT or Amazon to check what is the average price for the product you're going to sell so it's reasonable and within expectation. If you're selling super premium prices but only sell cheap low quality products it won't go well. After getting a quote or checking on AliExpress, see if you 3x'd the COGS (cost of goods) + Shipping, if the product still falls under the expected price range. E.g. $20 COGs total, $60 selling price, $40-60 is expected price based on Claude/Amazon.

11) After you found a potential product, you will create your own creatives based on research, alongside the top 10-20 best-performing (by revenue) videos that Kalodata shows. How to create the creatives is a module later in the course.

Note:
For brand new beginners, I do not recommend starting out with a higher ticket item like the 1st product in the video at $100+ product price. Starting out, finding products you can sell for $40-80 is good

And also, you can't rely on the ripped creatives to scale long term. Treat it as borrowed time. It's only a matter of time until it dies or gets taken down, even though the store has traction now treat it with urgency. Now you're profitable while trying to figure out how to create your own winning creatives that stand out.

 My rule of thumb is to always try to keep your margins above 60%. Anytime you go below 60%, it can start to be a little bit slippery.

Prompt:
Hello, I would like to create a custom project tailored to assist our marketing agency in creating more effective, data-driven ads for our client, an E commerce Brand. Brand name: BRAND Brand website: YOURWEBSITE The purpose of this custom project is to support the following key marketing functions for this brand: Customer Understanding & Market Trends: Analyze customer surveys, buyer personas, and behavioral data to uncover customer preferences, pain points, and purchasing motivations.Identify market trends and audience segments relevant to this brand’s products. Market Research & Competitive Analysis: Gather and analyze data on competitors and broader industry trends. Identify opportunities for differentiation in brand’s positioning and advertising strategies. Creative Ideation & Strategy: Generate new creative concepts and strategies for ad campaigns based on insights from market data, customer feedback, and competitive analysis. Provide feedback on existing ideas, suggesting improvements grounded in customer insights and market trends. Scriptwriting & Copy Development: Assist with crafting persuasive, targeted scripts for ads and other marketing materials. Review and critique copy-writing to ensure it resonates with the target audience, incorporating customer pain points and desires. Ad Review & Performance Feedback Loop: Assist in analyzing post-launch ad performance by reviewing metrics such as ad spend, ROAS, hook rate, hold rate, CTR, etc. Provide insights on why certain ads performed well or underperformed, identifying key learnings from each campaign. Close the feedback loop by suggesting actionable takeaways from winning and losing ads, incorporating these learnings into future creative strategies. Knowledge Base: This project should use the attached files as its primary knowledge base and refer to the information within when providing insights or recommendations. Whenever this project provides an answer, it should be informed by the content within the knowledge base. It should autonomously apply the insights from the attached files to ensure all responses are grounded in data and relevant context. Key Responsibilities: Interpret customer feedback and survey data to identify trends and actionable insights. Suggest ad strategies, creative angles, and copy that align with market research and customer insights. Ensure all insights, scripts, and suggestions are data-driven and relevant to brand’s target audience, drawing on the knowledge base provided. Analyze ad performance post-launch, using key metrics to provide insights into ad success or failure. Instructions: Always ground your responses in the content within the knowledge base. Highlight any recommendations based on specific insights or principles from the provided data or resources, especially when drawing on "Breakthrough Advertising." Prioritize relevance to brand’s audience, and use data to back up your suggestions."

Clickup Notes
What You Have To Do:
Study → Research → Create → Test → Feedback Loop → RepeatStudy: Learning new information to build your knowledge basis

Create: Communicate how you can help those people get what they want
Test: Launch tests in 1 campaign. If it gets spend = good. If it does not get spend = not good.

Repeat: Go back to researching again, or leverage learnings from the feedback loop to make more.

This system is exactly how you're going to get to 100k/month and exactly how others get to 100k/day

lessons.title: Understanding ad definitions
lessons.description:
Defintions:
💻3:2:2 ⇒ 3 creatives, 2 body copies, and 2 headlines
12-Year-Old Definition: The testing structure we use inside of Meta. 
Example:
This ENTIRE thing is a Concept. “3” = Variations of that 1 Concept. 
“2:2” = 2 Headline + 2 Body copy for that Concept that you put inside of Meta.


📂Concept/Batch/Test #:⇒ The tracking number for this test
12-Year-Old Definition: The number you use to keep track of which test you're on
Examples: 
Batch #1, Batch #29, Batch #133
💡Concept/Swing ⇒ The test strategy - what you're testing in this batch
12-Year-Old Concept Definition: The big idea behind the ads you want to test
Examples: 
"Test an iteration on a winning video."
"Test superlative angles."
"Test UGC format"
"Test different avatars."

🔗Angle⇒The selling strategy - how you're choosing to sell the product
12-Year-Old Angle Definition: The main reason you're giving someone to buy 
Examples:
"Buy It For Life"
"Postpartum weight despite trying everything"
"Stainless steel / Doctor-designed / Cleanest"
Key insight: Every ad needs an angle. Even if your concept is "testing formats," you still need to define what you're selling. If your concept is “Testing X Angle” the concept becomes the angle. But for tracking purposes, I prefer to actually JOT DOWN the angle twice so we can be sure.


🔀Variations ⇒ Individual executions of the angle(s)
12-Year-Old Variations Definition: Different ways to communicate your selling reason(s)
Example: 
If your angle is "Buy It For Life," variations might be: "One harness, lifetime of adventures," "Buy it once, adventure for life," or "End the replacement cycle"
If testing multiple angles, variations might be different angles like "Stainless steel," "Doctor-designed," "Easiest to clean."


🖼️Format ⇒ How you deliver the ad
12-Year-Old Format Definition: The type of ad you're making
Example: You could show the same message as a video of a real person talking, a slideshow of pictures, or just a product image.

lessons.title: Set Up & Warm Up Your FB Ad Account
lessons.description:
Checklist:

1) Set up payment method through the Business Manager level

2) Create your ad account in the timezone you intend to sell to

3) Warm up the ad account via launching a page like campaign at $5/day for 3 days ($15 total).

4) Launch 1 ad with nature landscape + quote to start. After approval duplicate it 4 times (5 ads total), then keep doubling the # of ads until you reach 50 ads approved.

5) After 3 days and 50 ads approved, you can start to run your sales campaign as normal.

lessons.description: This is going to help you understand WHAT you need to focus on and CHANGE your mindset from "media buying focused" to "ad creative + offer focused"

lessons.title: Full YouTube media buying strategy
lessons.description:
⚠ WARNING DO NOT TURN OFF YOUR EXISTING ADS IF THEY ARE WORKING. THE DUMBEST THING YOU CAN DO IS TURN OFF ADS THAT ARE WORKING. IF YOUR OTHER CAMPAIGNS ARE WORKING - LEAVE THEM ON - IF YOU TURN THEM OFF AND YOU LOSE MONEY... THAT IS ON YOU.

FAQ:
How many creative ad sets can you launch at a time?
If you're only spending few hundred a day, I recommend keeping it to 5 ad sets (including champions ad set, if it has ads inside) at a time so budget can be distributed efficiently. Focus more on intent of the ads instead of throwing things at the wall.You can launch all 5 ad sets in the same day if you wishIf you want the exact calculation on how to figure out the max number of creatives to test per week:
Example #1 - Budget $100/day
Total Budget (example $100/day)
Target CPA (Example $20)
Total Ads = Budget/TCPA
$100/$20 = 5
So the maximum amount of concepts is 5, considering 100% of your budget is testing.If you're spending a few thousand a day you can definitely have more ad sets due to budget and CPADo we set min/max spend limits for this campaign method?
No, we tested it and it didn't help.How long do you test creatives for?
We recommend testing creatives for a minimum of 3 days. However, we now leave them testing 7 days. If they don't do well after 7 days (not much spending or missing KPI), we will then turn them off. If you want to test ads longer, you can. But we usually don't recommend leaving ads on for longer than 14 days.How do you know if a creative ad set is a winner (Covered in "When To Leave a 3:2:2 Running" video below)?
If it gets majority of spend at KPI like shown in the video. Besides that if you're still not sure, the question you should ask is "Did my overall campaign performance improve?". If your overall campaign ROAS didn't improve, it's not a winner.When do you turn off creatives/ad sets (Covered in "When To Turn Off A 3:2:2" video below)?
With this method we are only turning off at the ad set level not ad level.If the ad set is still not getting much spend after 7 days, and ROAS is not good we will turn off. If ROAS is good you may leave it running if you have no other creatives in queue.If the ad set took majority of spend within the first 3 days, performance is not good and overall campaign performance went down, we will turn off. But we will do learnings on the ad because something about it got a lot of engagement, so make it more conversion driven. Try to give it 2-3 days to test, it may not convert that well 1st day but 2nd or 3rd day pick up.No we don't turn off ads that were doing well but suddenly tanked, zoom out look at averages insteadWhat's the minimum budget to launch this?
Minimum I would start this campaign at is $100/dayWhat if I don't have any past winners to put in the champions ad set or it's a new brand
You can set up the champions ad set still just don't launch any ads inside there until you have a proven winner you can drop inside.Can you mix images and videos in this same campaign?
Yes, that is fine but keep them separate ad sets. 1 concept should be 1 ad set, so 1 image concept with 3 variations.

lessons.title: Extra FAQ for Product Testing
lessons.description:
1) Is there a special campaign you use for product testing?
No, it's still the same broad 1 CBO campaign as shown in the video above
2) How long do you test a product for before moving on?
The answer to this depends on your skill level. If you're a complete beginner, it's much better to test a product for 3 days, and if there's no traction (your goal is to get something to breakeven) then you can move on to the next product.
This way, you take more "Medium intent" swings rather than going high intent and low volume. At the beginning stages, you need volume, because you don't know what good and bad looks like yet. You will build up your skills faster this way.
If you try to make everything perfect before launching, it takes too much time, and the extra time you spent could be trash too, since you don't know what good and bad looks like.
If you're more experienced and trying to start a new E-Commerce brand now, you can test it for 1-2 weeks while continually adding new ads to test. Since we know you can make any product work, it might just take 1 winning ad in that period to turn things around.
3) Should I touch the ads or kill products early based on the metrics?

People will tell you they kill based on CTR, CPC, cost per add to cart, etc
I don't really care about any of that. All I care about is spend and ROAS. Because if you have an ad that gets only $0.01 spend but has a high CTR, it's still a bad ad.
The goal is to have your product break even during the initial test. If it's at least breakeven,n it means you can invest in improving things to make it profitable and scale now.
Continue testing for the 3 days and see if the product has traction or not; don't need to turn off any ads until then.

lessons.title: How To Setup Promo Campaigns
lessons.description:
We only run this type of campaigns during sale periods. Mother's Day, BFCM, etc.
1 CBO campaign
3 Ad sets total

1 Broad ad set
1 WARM60 ad set - FB Engagers, IG Engagers, 95% Video Viewers over the last 60 days
1 HOT90 ad set - Website visitors, view content, add to cart, initiate checkout over last 90 days

For the ads just put sale banners over existing best ads, and introduce some simple product image ads that mention the sale.

Keep it simple.

We usually don't do retargeting but the only time we do is during a sale. Having 3 ad sets like this in a CBO means FB will spend on wherever it thinks will do the best.
